verdicts.cited_a: Deep discounts were used to move slow-selling inventory.
verdicts.cited_b: The reported profitability rate slipped year over year.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT: Side A names an activity (promotional discounting of slow-selling inventory); Side B names a financial outcome metric (profitability rate/margin), not the same underlying thing. Fails MECHANISM: gross margin decline is a downstream financial result that could stem from many causes (discounting, input costs, mix shift, labor) -- the quote never ties the margin slip specifically to promotional discounting, so B reads as a correlated/downstream effect rather than the same causal driver as A. SCOPE is also unstated on both sides (no segment, no company-owned vs franchised distinction). Since object and mechanism are not established as the same, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Stage length gives the average miles flown per trip.
verdicts.cited_b: Dwell time gives the average minutes a party occupies its table.
verdicts.reason: Fails all three tests: different object (miles flown per flight trip vs. minutes a party occupies a table), different scope (airline industry flight trips vs. restaurant industry table occupancy — unrelated business populations), and different mechanism (route/flight-distance driver vs. table-turnover driver). No compelling evidence of sameness, so default to DIFFERENT applies.

verdicts.cited_a: Do-it-yourself customers shop the counter and shelves directly.
verdicts.cited_b: Retail transactions exclude the professional installer business.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both are counter-transaction sales/purchase amounts in auto parts retail (A: "Purchases by walk-in consumers...grew"; B: "Amounts from everyday shoppers at the counter rose"). SCOPE: Side B pins "retail" to mean the non-professional consumer segment ("Retail transactions exclude the professional installer business"), which is precisely the population Side A describes ("walk-in consumers fixing their own cars," "Do-it-yourself customers shop the counter and shelves directly") — in the auto_parts industry, "Retail" and "DIY" are the standard paired label for the same non-commercial, non-installer customer class (as opposed to Pro/Commercial/DIFM). Both anchor to the same physical channel ("the counter"). MECHANISM: both describe direct growth in transactions/purchases by this same customer class, not a downstream or correlated effect. All three prongs hold, so despite different labels, this is the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: An add-on fee to recover pricier fuel was applied.
verdicts.cited_b: A levy offsetting higher energy cost was charged.
verdicts.reason: Object/mechanism are only loosely aligned: A names the input as "fuel" specifically, B says "energy cost" - a broader category that isn't confirmed to mean jet fuel rather than some other energy input, so OBJECT sameness isn't compelled, just plausible. Fatal gap is SCOPE: neither quote (nor slice_tokens/per_x, both empty/null) says what business population or ownership class the charge applies to - no mention of consolidated vs segment, gross vs net, or which routes/services are covered. With no verbatim text backing scope sameness, that criterion is unclear, and per the rule any unclear criterion forces DIFFERENT. Defaulting to DIFFERENT per the over-splitting-is-cheap principle.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross merchandise value counts all marketplace orders.
verdicts.cited_b: Net revenue counts only the commission we retain.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and SCOPE. Side A measures total marketplace order value (gross, all sellers' volume); Side B measures only the commission the company keeps (net share) — an explicit gross vs. net split the rubric treats as a scope break. The mechanisms also differ: GMV moves with total transaction volume across the marketplace, while net revenue moves with the take-rate/commission retained, a downstream slice of that volume, not the same causal driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Operating margin is profit over revenue after corporate.
verdicts.cited_b: Restaurant-level margin is profit over revenue before corporate.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT: the quotes explicitly define these as complementary, not identical, metrics -- operating margin nets profit "after corporate" (corporate overhead deducted), while restaurant-level margin is profit "before corporate" (corporate overhead excluded). Also fails SCOPE: operating margin reflects the consolidated/company-wide population including corporate costs, whereas restaurant-level margin is scoped to unit-level restaurant economics before corporate allocation. Since the two metrics differ by definition on what is netted out, MECHANISM also diverges (corporate cost drivers affect one but not the other). None of the three required conditions is met, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Wages, benefits and taxes combine into staffing cost.
verdicts.cited_b: Only the employer levy sits in this smaller line.
verdicts.reason: Object test fails: Side A is a composite aggregate ("staffing cost" = wages + benefits + taxes combined), while Side B is explicitly a narrower single component ("Only the employer levy" in a "smaller line"). The word "Only" and "smaller line" signal Side B deliberately excludes wages and benefits, which Side A includes. This is a whole-vs-part relationship (payroll tax expense is one ingredient inside labor and related expense), not the same underlying object or mechanism. Since object identity is not established (and is in fact contradicted), default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross passenger revenue is stated before taxes collected.
verdicts.cited_b: Net passenger revenue is stated after taxes remitted.
verdicts.reason: Fails SCOPE: the grading rubric explicitly lists "gross vs net" as a disqualifying scope mismatch, and that is exactly the distinction here — Side A is passenger revenue before taxes are collected, Side B is passenger revenue after taxes are remitted, i.e. two different reported figures (different tax treatment/base), not one shared population/measurement basis. Since SCOPE fails, the pair cannot be SAME regardless of OBJECT or MECHANISM overlap.

verdicts.cited_a: Shipping bottlenecks delayed inbound merchandise flow.
verdicts.cited_b: Empty shelves rose across several important categories.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A describes a logistics/transportation process (freight and shipping delays affecting inbound goods movement). Side B describes an inventory-availability outcome (empty shelves at the point of sale). Out-of-stock shelves are a downstream symptom that could result from shipping delays, but equally from demand spikes, poor allocation, or supplier production shortfalls -- it is a correlated/downstream effect, not the same causal mechanism as freight bottlenecks. No quote on either side establishes that the empty shelves were specifically caused by the port/freight delays, so the causal link is asserted, not compelled by the evidence. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Same-restaurant sales compare restaurants open in both periods.
verdicts.cited_b: Comparable restaurant sales compare restaurants open both periods.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both quotes define the identical metric — a sales comparison for restaurants open in both periods — worded almost verbatim identically ("restaurants open in both periods" vs "restaurants open both periods"). MECHANISM: same causal logic on both sides (excluding newly opened/closed restaurants to isolate organic sales performance) — no divergence in how the comparison is constructed. SCOPE: neither side qualifies the population by ownership class (no company-owned vs franchised, no segment vs consolidated) — both say plain "restaurants" with empty slice_tokens and null per_x, so there is no textual signal of a scope difference. "Same-restaurant sales" and "comparable restaurant sales" are standard industry-synonym labels for one metric; the evidence compels treating them as the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Cattle market swings drive this primary protein input.
verdicts.cited_b: Poultry supply cycles move a separate menu ingredient.

Different MECHANISM: cattle market swings vs poultry supply cycles are separate, uncorrelated commodity price drivers. Side B's own quote explicitly labels chicken as "a separate menu ingredient" from beef, confirming these are distinct causes, not a shared driver expressed differently. Fails object and mechanism identity, so default to DIFFERENT.

A segment-level subset is not the same population as the company-wide total, so this is a segment-vs-consolidated mismatch, not the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Fares plus onboard charges make up the broad figure.
verdicts.cited_b: Just the choose-your-seat fee feeds this narrow slice.
verdicts.reason: Fails same OBJECT and same SCOPE. Side A is explicitly a broad aggregate ("Fares plus onboard charges... the broad figure") while Side B is explicitly a narrow single line item ("Just the choose-your-seat fee... this narrow slice"). Seat selection revenue is at most a small sub-component of total passenger revenue, not the same metric or population — one is the whole, the other is a narrow slice of it. No mechanism equivalence can be established when the scope itself differs this starkly, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Our like-for-like sales at stores open more than a year rose modestly.
verdicts.cited_b: Sales at outlets operating for more than twelve months improved on stronger do-it-yourself demand.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both describe the identical metric — the standard comparable/same-store sales figure. A: "like-for-like sales at stores open more than a year"; B: "Sales at outlets operating for more than twelve months" — "like-for-like" = "comparable," and "a year" = "twelve months," so this is one metric definition in two phrasings, not two different metrics. SCOPE: both name the same population with no conflicting ownership/segment qualifier — A's population is "stores open more than a year," B's is "outlets operating for more than twelve months"; neither quote carves out a franchise-vs-company-owned or segment-vs-consolidated distinction, so there is no textual signal of a scope mismatch, and both sit in the same industry ("auto_parts"). MECHANISM: both attribute a change in overall results to the same causal driver — growth in sales at the existing (>1yr) store base — A: sales "rose modestly," B: sales "improved." B adds color on the demand source ("stronger do-it-yourself demand") but that explains WHY comp sales moved, it does not substitute a different driver; the cited causal factor in both is comp-store-sales performance itself, not a downstream or merely correlated effect. All three prongs hold with verbatim support, so this is a genuine match rather than an over-merge.

verdicts.cited_a: Too many seats chased limited demand on key routes.
verdicts.cited_b: Unit revenue declined 4 percent as fares weakened on oversupplied routes.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A describes the supply-side root cause: excess seat capacity versus demand ("Too many seats chased limited demand on key routes"). Side B describes a downstream financial outcome: revenue per seat/fares weakening. Critically, B's own quote frames itself as a consequence of A's condition ("Unit revenue declined 4 percent as fares weakened on oversupplied routes") — this is the correlated/downstream-effect case the rubric explicitly excludes from "same mechanism." Capacity oversupply is a cause; unit revenue decline is its result, not the same underlying driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Value on prepaid cards never spent flowed to income.
verdicts.cited_b: Balances customers left behind boosted the top line.
verdicts.reason: Object: both describe money loaded onto prepaid/gift cards that customers never redeem ("prepaid cards never spent" vs "balances customers left behind") -- same underlying thing, gift-card breakage. Scope: both are restaurants industry with no slice_tokens or per_x given, and neither quote signals a narrower population (no franchised-vs-company-owned or segment-vs-consolidated split) -- same scope by default. Mechanism: both describe the identical causal path -- unredeemed balance converts into recognized income/revenue because it's never spent/redeemed (breakage) -- "flowed to income" and "boosted the top line" are just paraphrases of the same breakage-recognition mechanism, not a correlated or downstream effect. All three criteria hold, so this is over-splitting risk rather than genuine difference; approve SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Unredeemed prepaid cards sit as an obligation on the books.
verdicts.cited_b: Redemption and breakage turn cards into earned income.
verdicts.reason: Object differs: A is the outstanding liability balance (unredeemed cards as an obligation); B is earned income/sales recognized upon redemption or breakage — a balance-sheet item vs. an income-statement item. Scope is unstated/unclear on both sides. Mechanism differs: A grows when purchases outpace redemptions (liability accrual); B is driven by redemption/breakage events converting that liability into revenue — these are inverse/complementary mechanisms, not the same causal driver. Since object and mechanism both fail, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Charges travelers pay to check luggage added up.
verdicts.cited_b: Income from stowed suitcase charges contributed.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: "charges...to check luggage" (A) and "stowed suitcase charges" (B) describe the identical thing - fees for checked/stowed baggage, just paraphrased. SCOPE: both are airlines-wide with no slice_tokens or per_x, no indication of different populations (e.g., no segment/ownership split) - same undifferentiated scope. MECHANISM: both describe the fee income itself as the driver ("added up" / "contributed" to results), not one being a downstream/correlated effect of the other - same direct causal mechanism (checked-bag fee revenue). All three criteria hold, so this is a genuine paraphrase pair, not a true difference.

verdicts.cited_a: Fuel spend divided by gallons gives this per-gallon price.
verdicts.cited_b: Consumption and price together set the expense line.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is a per-unit rate (fuel spend / gallons = price per gallon), an isolated price metric used for hedging decisions. Side B is an aggregate dollar total ("Fuel spend summed for the period gives the total expense") that is explicitly driven by two combined factors -- "Consumption and price together set the expense line" -- i.e. volume (gallons consumed) multiplied by price. Total expense can move from consumption changes alone even with flat per-gallon price, so the two metrics respond to different causal levers; per-gallon cost isolates price, total expense conflates price with volume. Since the object (unit rate vs. aggregate spend) and mechanism (price-only vs. volume*price) both differ, this fails the same-OBJECT and same-MECHANISM tests, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: European sourcing shifts with the single-currency cross.
verdicts.cited_b: Overall import buying power follows a trade-weighted basket.
verdicts.reason: Object differs: Side A is a single bilateral EUR/USD cross rate; Side B is a broad trade-weighted basket index spanning many currencies, not just the euro. Scope differs: Side A is scoped to "European sourcing" specifically, while Side B covers "overall import buying power" across all sourcing geographies. Mechanism differs: A moves via one currency pair's cross rate, B moves via a weighted average across a basket -- the euro is at most one input among several to B, so a shift in A need not move B proportionally. All three required matches fail, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted gross margin excludes purchase-accounting amounts.
verdicts.cited_b: Gross margin includes those purchase-accounting amounts.
verdicts.reason: Fails same OBJECT/MECHANISM: the two quotes explicitly define the metrics as differing by construction — Side A is a non-GAAP adjusted figure that strips out purchase-accounting amounts, while Side B is the reported figure that retains them. This isn't a correlated or downstream relationship, it's a direct definitional exclusion vs inclusion of the same cost component, so the two will diverge whenever purchase-accounting amounts are material (e.g., post-acquisition periods). Adjusted metrics are constructed precisely to tell a different story than their unadjusted counterparts, so they are not the same underlying driver. Default-to-DIFFERENT applies since sameness is not compelled.

verdicts.cited_a: Unfilled appliance commitments stayed elevated at close.
verdicts.cited_b: Recognition follows install and handoff to the household.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM (and SCOPE is unclear). Side A describes an operational metric - the stock of unfilled customer orders/commitments for appliances (a backlog/queue). Side B describes an accounting policy - the timing trigger for revenue recognition (upon install/handoff). These are different objects: one is orders-not-yet-fulfilled, the other is a recognition-timing rule. Mechanistically they differ too: backlog levels are driven by demand vs. fulfillment/supply capacity, while revenue-on-delivery is a GAAP/accounting-policy mechanism about when a sale converts to booked revenue. At best backlog is a downstream/correlated proxy for revenue not-yet-recognized under a deliver-to-recognize policy, but a correlated or downstream relationship is explicitly excluded from counting as 'same mechanism.' Scope also doesn't clearly match: Side A is scoped to 'appliance' commitments specifically, while Side B's quote makes no product-line restriction, just 'install and handoff to the household' generally. With object and mechanism both failing, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Available seat miles are seats flown times the miles flown.
verdicts.cited_b: ASMs are seats flown times the miles flown, abbreviated.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: identical definition verbatim ("seats flown times the miles flown") — ASMs is explicitly the abbreviation of available seat miles, same metric. MECHANISM: same causal driver — flown capacity (seats × miles), not a correlated or downstream effect. All three criteria hold with verbatim support, so this is a full-name/abbreviation duplicate rather than a distinct driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Same-store sales growth compares against the prior year.

verdicts.reason: Mechanism differs: Side A measures growth against the same period one year ago (a same-store/comparable metric that strips out seasonality by design), while Side B measures growth against the immediately prior quarter and explicitly attributes the move to seasonality ("rose 1.1 percent on seasonality"). A year-over-year comparable-store comparison and a quarter-over-quarter sequential comparison are different causal computations, not the same underlying driver restated. Object/scope are also unclear to be equal (A is scoped to "same-store"/comparable units; B does not establish that same-store restriction, so it may include broader sales). Since MECHANISM clearly fails (and OBJECT/SCOPE are not established as matching), this defaults to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Reported revenue is measured at actual exchange rates.
verdicts.cited_b: Constant-currency revenue is measured at fixed rates.
verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT (revenue) and no evidence of different scope, but MECHANISM fails: reported revenue's mechanism includes actual currency translation effects ("actual exchange rates," "including currency headwinds"), while constant-currency revenue's mechanism explicitly strips currency out ("fixed rates"). This is not a rounding difference -- it changes the growth figure from 4 percent to 7 percent, meaning a real causal component (FX movement) is present in one and deliberately removed from the other. Since the two numbers are driven by different underlying causes (one includes an FX effect, the other excludes it), same-mechanism is false, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Guest counts improved for the first time in six quarters.
verdicts.cited_b: More diners came through our doors even as the industry stayed promotional.

On MECHANISM, Side A is a bare metric statement with no causal context ("improved for the first time in six quarters"), while Side B frames the gain against a competitive backdrop ("even as the industry stayed promotional"), implying a share-gain-despite-discounting story that Side A gives no basis to confirm or deny. With two of the three required elements unclear rather than compelled by the evidence, the pair fails the bar for SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Persistent tightness in hourly hiring pushed starting wages up again across most trade areas.

Scope is also unstated/unclear on both sides (no ownership-class or population match confirmed). Since wage inflation is at most one possible contributor to labor-expense growth rather than a proven identical mechanism, and object/scope are not confirmed to match, this fails the same-object and same-mechanism tests -- default to DIFFERENT.

Since the underlying metric definitions differ by construction (not just by scope or mechanism), this is not the same object, so no further check is needed -> DIFFERENT.

A ratio/rate and its own numerator aggregate are not the same underlying driver. Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_b: Restaurant sales summed across guests gives the total.

Side A is a per-guest average (sales ÷ guests, per_x="guest") reflecting price/mix per transaction; Side B is an aggregate total (sales summed across guests, per_x=null) reflecting overall volume, which conflates guest traffic with average check. Total sales = average check × guest count, so total sales is a downstream product, not the same causal driver as average check. Same industry/population is not enough when the object and mechanism differ -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: These expenses cover store payroll, advertising and overhead.
verdicts.cited_b: SG&A expense covers store payroll, advertising and overhead.
verdicts.reason: Object: both quotes are near-verbatim identical ("store payroll, advertising and overhead"), and "selling_general_and_administrative_expenses" is simply the spelled-out form of "sga_expense" -- same line item. Scope: both are retail-industry, and both explicitly span store-level and corporate/overhead costs (A: "corporate overhead as well as store-level administration"; B: "occupancy support and payroll administration") with no conflicting population/ownership signal (no franchise-vs-owned or segment-vs-consolidated split indicated on either side). Mechanism: both describe the same cost composition driving the line (payroll, advertising, overhead/occupancy) with no divergent causal driver. All three tests pass, so SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: How often diners added a drink to the order rose.
verdicts.cited_b: The rate of beverages sold alongside meals climbed.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: A's "added a drink to the order" and B's "beverages sold alongside meals" describe the identical metric — the frequency of a beverage accompanying a food order (attach rate = incidence rate, just different vocabulary). SCOPE: neither side has slice_tokens or per_x; both are generic "restaurants" industry with no ownership-class or segment qualifier, so scope is consistent (unscoped, overall order-level). MECHANISM: A's "Attachment improved with combo pricing changes" and B's "Incidence rose as bundles gained traction" cite the same causal driver — combo/bundle pricing deals increasing beverage attach. All three tests (object, scope, mechanism) are satisfied with direct textual support, so this clears the bar for SAME despite the default-to-DIFFERENT prior.

verdicts.cited_a: Diners held tables through our platform before arriving.
verdicts.cited_b: The register captures amounts at the moment guests pay.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A counts reservations/table-holds made in advance through a booking platform (a pre-visit demand/capacity signal). Side B records sales revenue captured at the point of payment (a post-visit financial transaction). A booked reservation is not a completed sale (no-shows/cancellations exist) and not every sale originates from a reservation (walk-ins exist) - reservations booked is at most an upstream, correlated precursor to dine-in sales, not the same causal mechanism or the same object being measured. Scope also differs: platform reservation-makers vs. register-paying guests. Since not all three criteria are clearly satisfied, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Restaurant sales divided by units gives this per-store rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Restaurant sales summed across units gives the total.
verdicts.reason: Fails same OBJECT and same MECHANISM. Side A is a per-store average (sales divided by unit count, i.e. average unit volume) — a rate that isolates per-store performance. Side B is an aggregate total (sales summed across all units) — a total that mechanically rises with more store openings even if per-store performance is flat or falling. Division vs summation are different mathematical objects with different causal mechanisms (per-unit productivity vs. total footprint/unit-count growth), confirmed by per_x differing (\"restaurant\" vs null). Default-to-DIFFERENT applies since sameness is not compelled.

verdicts.cited_a: Capacity measures available seat miles across the fleet.
verdicts.cited_b: Capacity measures throughput across the distribution fleet.
verdicts.reason: Fails all three tests. Object: Side A is airline seat-miles (passenger seating output) vs Side B is retail distribution throughput (goods movement) -- different underlying metrics. Scope: Side A is airlines industry vs Side B is retail industry -- different business populations entirely. Mechanism: seat-mile capacity is driven by aircraft size/scheduling while distribution throughput is driven by logistics/warehouse network flow -- unrelated causal drivers. The shared name "capacity" and the word "fleet" in both quotes are surface-level coincidences (airplane fleet vs delivery fleet), not evidence of a shared driver. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The central bank lifted its target several times.
verdicts.cited_b: The reported cost of borrowings climbed accordingly.
verdicts.reason: Object differs (central bank policy rate vs. a company's reported cost of borrowings) and scope differs (macro/economy-wide vs. company-level financials). Most decisively, mechanism fails: Side B's own quote ("...climbed accordingly") frames it as a downstream effect of Side A's rate hikes, not the same causal driver -- the rubric explicitly excludes downstream/correlated effects from counting as same mechanism.

verdicts.cited_a: Fares earned for each seat carried a mile defined the rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Passenger fares earned per mile of capacity set the figure.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both describe a unit-revenue rate — fares earned divided by a capacity-mile measure (A: "fares earned for each seat carried a mile"; B: "passenger fares earned per mile of capacity"). SCOPE: neither quote restricts to a sub-segment, ownership class, or gross/net split (no slice_tokens on either side; both industry=airlines) — both read as the whole-business fares-per-capacity figure. MECHANISM: "mile of capacity" is standard airline shorthand for available seat miles (capacity = ASMs, distinct from revenue-passenger-miles/traffic), so B's "fares per mile of capacity" is the same causal computation as A's "fares per seat carried a mile" (fares ÷ ASM) — not a correlated or downstream metric but the identical rate construction, just paraphrased (name-level "unit_revenue" is the generic industry label for RASM/revenue-per-ASM described by A).

verdicts.cited_a: Total revenue includes company sales and franchise royalties.
verdicts.cited_b: Total revenues include company sales and franchise royalties.
verdicts.reason: Object: both describe the identical metric "total revenue(s)" (singular/plural naming variant only). Scope: both combine the same population and ownership classes -- company-owned sales plus franchise royalties -- into one consolidated total, same industry (restaurants), no slice_tokens/per_x differentiation on either side. Mechanism: both state the total is literally composed of company sales + franchise royalties, the same causal composition, not a correlated/downstream effect. Quotes are near-verbatim identical, compelling SAME.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted operating margin excludes non-representative items.
verdicts.cited_b: Operating margin includes every item under the rules.
verdicts.reason: Fails the same-OBJECT test. Side A's quote defines adjusted operating margin as explicitly EXCLUDING non-representative items, while Side B's quote defines operating margin as INCLUDING every item under the rules. These are stated as opposite treatments of items (a non-GAAP adjusted measure vs. the unadjusted/GAAP measure), so they are different metrics by definition, not the same underlying object. Since the exclusions are exactly what separates the two figures, the mechanism also cannot be assumed identical. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Average unit volume is average annual sales per restaurant.
verdicts.cited_b: AUV is average annual sales per restaurant, abbreviated.
verdicts.reason: Same OBJECT: both quotes define the metric identically as "average annual sales per restaurant" - AUV is explicitly the abbreviation of average unit volume. Same SCOPE: both use per_x=restaurant, industry=restaurants, with no slice_tokens distinguishing franchised vs company-owned or any other subpopulation - identical scope. Same MECHANISM: both describe the same causal driver (per-restaurant sales productivity), not a correlated or downstream effect. All three criteria hold with verbatim support, so this is a name/abbreviation duplicate, not a distinct driver.

verdicts.cited_a: Comps means comparable-store sales at open stores.
verdicts.cited_b: Comps means complimentary meals given to guests.
verdicts.reason: Same name ("comps") but different OBJECT entirely: Side A's comps is a sales-performance metric (comparable-store sales at open stores, auto parts), while Side B's comps is free food given to guests (complimentary meals, restaurants). These are unrelated underlying things -- a revenue metric vs. a giveaway/expense item -- so OBJECT fails, which alone forces DIFFERENT (SCOPE and MECHANISM also fail since there is no shared population or causal driver between a sales comparison and a meal giveaway).

verdicts.cited_a: Salon services revenue reflects in-store salon activity.
verdicts.cited_b: Total net sales reflect merchandise and services company-wide.
verdicts.reason: Fails same-OBJECT and same-SCOPE. Side A is a specific revenue line item limited to in-store salon services activity. Side B is the total aggregate of merchandise AND services across the whole company. Salon services revenue is at most a subset/component of total net sales, not the same metric — one is a narrow service-line figure, the other is a company-wide grand total spanning merchandise plus all services. Since the object (line-item vs. total aggregate) and scope (salon-only subset vs. entire company, merchandise+services) both diverge, this is a clear part-to-whole relationship, not driver identity. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Shrink stepped up again, concentrated in high-theft categories.
verdicts.cited_b: Inventory shortage results worsened, reflecting losses discovered at physical counts.
verdicts.reason: Object is plausibly shared (both describe unaccounted inventory loss) and scope isn't contradicted, but mechanism is not confirmed identical: A explicitly attributes the rise to theft ("concentrated in high-theft categories"), while B only states losses were "discovered at physical counts" — a measurement/detection method, not a stated cause. B's quote leaves the actual driver (theft vs. damage vs. clerical error vs. vendor issues) unspecified, so the mechanism match is unclear. Per the rule, unclear mechanism defaults to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Long-dated government paper reflects distant expectations.
verdicts.cited_b: Short maturities track near-term policy bets instead.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is the 10-year yield (long-dated paper) and Side B is the 2-year yield (short maturities) -- different points on the yield curve, not the same instrument. Mechanistically they diverge too: Side A's quote ties the driver to distant/long-term expectations (growth, inflation over a decade), while Side B's quote ties its driver to near-term policy bets (Fed rate expectations). These are the classic case of long vs. short maturity yields moving on different, sometimes opposite, causal forces (e.g., yield-curve inversion), so sameness is not compelled -- default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The share of visitors who bought something ticked up.
verdicts.cited_b: How often a browser became a buyer improved.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: both describe the identical metric -- the proportion of people who came to look (visitor/browser) who ended up purchasing (bought something/became a buyer); "visitor" and "browser" are synonymous retail terms for the same population, not different funnel stages. SCOPE: both are unscoped retail (no slice_tokens, no per_x, no company-owned/franchise or segment split mentioned on either side) -- same business population and ownership class. MECHANISM: both quotes report the same causal movement -- an increase in the rate at which that population converts to buyers ("ticked up" / "improved" on the same visitor-to-buyer conversion) -- not a merely correlated or downstream effect. All three prongs hold, so SAME is compelled despite the different field names.

verdicts.cited_a: Mix reflects what guests ordered, as premium entrees displaced value items.
verdicts.cited_b: Mix reflects the blend of premium cabin and main cabin seats sold.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and SCOPE: Side A concerns restaurant menu item mix (premium entrees vs value items ordered by guests), while Side B concerns airline seat class mix (premium cabin vs main cabin seats sold) — entirely different industries (restaurants vs airlines), different underlying populations (food orders vs seat inventory), and no shared business object. Only superficial similarity is the word "mix" and a premium-vs-value pattern, which is not enough to establish sameness.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross margin dollars are revenue minus merchandise cost.
verdicts.cited_b: Net margin is income shown as a percentage of revenue.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is an absolute dollar figure equal to revenue minus merchandise cost only (i.e., gross profit, before operating expenses, interest, taxes). Side B is a percentage/ratio of income (net income) to revenue, which nets out all expenses, not just merchandise cost. Different units (dollars vs. percentage), different cost scope (merchandise cost only vs. full income after all expenses), and different causal mechanism (a subtraction vs. a full bottom-line ratio) — these are distinct financial metrics, not the same driver expressed differently. No scope detail (segment/ownership) is given for either side either, so criterion 2 is also unverified. Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Total pay and benefits for restaurant teams climbed with market wage moves.
verdicts.cited_b: Compensation for restaurant employees, all in, rose.
verdicts.reason: MECHANISM fails/is unclear: Side A explicitly attributes the rise to "market wage moves" (external labor-market wage inflation as the causal driver). Side B states only that compensation "rose" with no stated cause -- it could be from wage increases, headcount growth, benefit plan changes, overtime, or mix shift. Since Side B gives no mechanism at all, sameness of mechanism cannot be confirmed from the quote. Additionally, SCOPE is unclear: neither quote specifies ownership class (company-owned vs franchised restaurants), so the required scope match is not verifiable either. Per the rule, any unclear criterion forces DIFFERENT, so this does not qualify as SAME despite both quotes plausibly referring to the same OBJECT (total restaurant labor compensation).

verdicts.cited_a: Average spend rose to 19.40 dollars per guest.
verdicts.cited_b: Per-guest receipts averaged about 19.40 dollars.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: Both sides describe the identical metric -- revenue per guest -- and land on the exact same figure, $19.40 ("...19.40 dollars per guest" vs "...averaged about 19.40 dollars"). Side A's first quote ("how much a diner pays on a typical visit") and Side B's first quote ("total receipts divided across patrons served") describe the same per-guest computation (receipts / guests), not a per-check/per-ticket figure, so the "average_check" vs "average_spend_per_guest" naming difference is just two labels for the same underlying per-guest spend, confirmed by the matching dollar amount. SCOPE: neither side names a segment or ownership split (company-owned vs franchised, gross vs net); both are unqualified restaurant-industry figures with empty slice_tokens, so there is no scope conflict. MECHANISM: both simply report the same metric trending up ("ticked higher" / "edged upward") -- since it is the same object moving, this is the same measurement, not a correlated/downstream effect of a different driver. All three criteria hold.

verdicts.cited_a: Company-operated locations produced 2.1 billion dollars of sales.
verdicts.cited_b: Franchised locations produced 9.4 billion dollars of sales for operators.
verdicts.reason: Scope mismatch on ownership class: Side A is sales from company-operated (directly run) restaurants, while Side B is sales generated by independently owned franchised operators -- this is exactly the "company-owned vs franchised" example of a scope difference. Mechanism also differs: direct-operation revenue vs. independent-operator revenue attributed to franchisees. The very different magnitudes ($2.1B vs $9.4B) further confirm these are distinct populations, not the same driver under two labels.

verdicts.cited_a: Merchandise and freight together form the full amount.
verdicts.cited_b: Inbound transport alone makes up this narrow piece.
verdicts.reason: Object test fails: Side A (cost_of_sales) is explicitly the combined total of merchandise cost AND freight ("together form the full amount"), whereas Side B (freight_cost) is explicitly a single narrow component, inbound transport, taken "alone" as "this narrow piece." One is a whole aggregate, the other is a subset/component of that whole (freight is one input into cost of sales, alongside merchandise) -- they are not the same underlying metric, so this is a part-vs-whole relationship rather than sameness. Since OBJECT already fails, no need to reach scope or mechanism -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Gross franchise revenue includes ad-fund amounts collected.
verdicts.cited_b: Net franchise revenue excludes ad-fund amounts collected.
verdicts.reason: Fails the SCOPE test. The grading rubric explicitly names "gross vs net" as a scope distinction, and that is exactly what separates these two: gross franchise revenue includes ad-fund amounts collected, while net franchise revenue excludes ad-fund amounts collected. Same object label (franchise revenue) and industry, but they are measured on different bases (one includes a pass-through component the other strips out), so the underlying quantity being reported is not the same scope. Default-to-DIFFERENT applies since scope is explicitly different, not just unclear.

verdicts.cited_a: Profit as a share of revenue widened over the year.
verdicts.cited_b: Operating profit per dollar of revenue improved on cost discipline.
verdicts.reason: Object is only loosely aligned: A says unspecified "profit" as a share of revenue, B explicitly says "operating profit" per dollar of revenue — plausibly the same ratio but not confirmed identical from A's wording alone. Scope is unconfirmed on both sides: neither quote states the business population or ownership class (company-owned vs franchised, segment vs consolidated) — slice_tokens are empty for both. Mechanism fails outright: A gives no causal driver at all (just notes the metric "widened over the year"), while B attributes the change to "cost discipline." Since A cites no mechanism, sameness of mechanism cannot be verified by a quote from A. With scope unclear and mechanism unconfirmed, the evidence does not compel sameness, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Prices paid for core kitchen inputs pressured the quarter.
verdicts.cited_b: What we pay for menu inputs weighed on results.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT: "core kitchen inputs" (A) and "menu inputs" (B) both denote the same thing -- the raw food/ingredients a restaurant buys to prepare its menu; "ingredient_costs" and "food_costs" are synonymous terms in restaurant-industry usage. MECHANISM: both describe the identical causal chain -- prices paid for those inputs rising and directly pressuring/weighing on financial results (not a downstream or merely correlated effect). SCOPE: neither quote invokes any population or ownership qualifier (no company-owned vs franchised, no segment vs consolidated, no gross vs net), and both industries match (restaurants), so there is no signal of differing scope -- both read as whole-company statements. All three prongs are satisfied with no contradicting evidence, so this pair clears the bar for SAME despite the paraphrased wording.

verdicts.cited_a: Division operating profit reflects one brand's restaurants.
verdicts.cited_b: Consolidated operating profit reflects all brands combined.
verdicts.reason: Scope differs explicitly: Side A is single-brand/division-level ("one brand's restaurants"), while Side B is company-wide across all brands plus corporate costs ("all brands combined," "every brand plus corporate costs"). This fails the same-SCOPE test (division vs. consolidated ownership/population), so mechanism also differs (single-brand performance vs. aggregate multi-brand plus corporate overhead). Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The secured overnight rate anchors floating bank debt.
verdicts.cited_b: A policy target the central bank sets for reserves.
verdicts.reason: Fails object, mechanism, and likely scope. Object: SOFR is a secured, market-observed repo transaction rate, while the federal funds rate is an administered policy target rate -- different underlying instruments. Mechanism: SOFR emerges from actual collateralized market transactions; the fed funds rate is set by central bank policy decision (FOMC), not market transactions -- a policy mechanism vs a market mechanism. Scope: SOFR quote describes anchoring floating bank debt broadly, while the fed funds rate quote describes targeting reserves lending specifically. No verbatim evidence compels treating these as the same driver, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: How fast stock cycled through the stores improved.
verdicts.cited_b: The pace at which merchandise sold and refilled quickened.
verdicts.reason: Same object: both describe inventory turnover/turns (stock cycling vs. merchandise sold-and-refilled are the same metric, and "inventory_turns"/"inventory_turnover" are standard synonyms). Same scope: neither quote scopes to a sub-population -- no company-owned/franchised, segment/consolidated, or gross/net distinction in either; both carry industry:"retail" with empty slice_tokens and null per_x, consistently indicating the same unqualified, overall-retail population rather than a conflict. Same mechanism: both describe the identical causal driver -- the speed/pace of inventory cycling through the business increasing -- not a correlated or downstream effect.

verdicts.cited_a: EBITDA margin is earnings over revenue before charges.
verdicts.cited_b: Net margin is earnings over revenue after all charges.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT test fails: Side A's quote defines EBITDA margin as earnings over revenue "before charges" (excludes interest, tax, depreciation, amortization), while Side B's quote defines net margin as earnings over revenue "after all charges" (includes everything). These are two distinct, well-defined financial metrics with different inclusion sets, not the same metric described differently -- so this is a different OBJECT (and consequently a different MECHANISM: pre-charge operating profitability vs. post-all-charge bottom-line profitability). Default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Prepaid installs not yet performed remain a liability.
verdicts.cited_b: Completing the work moves amounts to earned income.
verdicts.reason: Different OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A describes a liability (unearned/deferred revenue balance) whose defining condition is that work has NOT yet been performed. Side B describes recognized revenue (an income-statement item) whose defining condition is that work HAS been completed. These are two distinct financial statement items (liability vs. earned income), not the same metric viewed from two angles, and the causal mechanisms are opposite (non-performance sustaining a liability vs. completion triggering recognition). No scope tokens are given to establish matching population/ownership class either. Since OBJECT sameness is not compelled by the quotes, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Guest traffic softened mid-quarter before recovering as weather normalized across dining rooms.
verdicts.cited_b: Store traffic trends improved sequentially as shoppers responded to sharper opening price points.
verdicts.reason: Object is only superficially shared (both called "traffic" but one counts restaurant guests, the other retail shoppers). Scope fails: restaurant dining rooms vs. retail stores are different industries and different business populations. Mechanism fails: Side A's traffic change is caused by weather normalizing, while Side B's is caused by shoppers reacting to lower opening price points -- unrelated causal drivers. With scope and mechanism both failing, default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted SG&A excludes transformation and severance costs.
verdicts.cited_b: SG&A expense includes transformation and severance costs.
verdicts.reason: Same-OBJECT test fails: the two quotes explicitly contrast treatment of identical cost items. Side A defines "Adjusted SG&A" as a non-GAAP measure that excludes transformation and severance costs, while Side B defines "SG&A expense" as the reported figure that includes those same costs. By construction these are two different metrics (adjusted vs. as-reported), not one driver described two ways, so the pair defaults to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Aircraft we fly ourselves carry this per-seat figure.
verdicts.cited_b: Capacity purchased from affiliates carries another.
verdicts.reason: Object matches (unit cost per available seat mile, i.e. CASM) but SCOPE fails: Side A covers aircraft the company flies itself (mainline/self-operated fleet), while Side B covers capacity purchased from affiliates (regional/purchased-capacity operations). This is the same ownership-class split called out in the rule as disqualifying (analogous to company-owned vs franchised) -- mainline and regional capacity have distinct cost structures (own-fleet operating costs vs capacity-purchase agreements), so the mechanism driving each unit-cost figure differs too. Defaulting to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: The line includes salaries, landing fees, rent, maintenance and depreciation.
verdicts.cited_b: Only fleet repair work lands in this narrower line.
verdicts.reason: Side A is the full aggregate cost line (salaries + landing fees + rent + maintenance + depreciation summed together), while Side B is explicitly a "narrower" sub-line covering only fleet repair/maintenance work. A's own quote lists "maintenance" as just one of several components rolled into the total, so B (aircraft maintenance) is a subset/component of A (total operating expenses), not the same thing. Object fails (aggregate vs. single component), scope fails (all cost categories vs. only repair work), and mechanism fails (summation across many cost drivers vs. fleet repair/heavy-check activity specifically) -> DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Total sales divided by store count gives this per-store rate.
verdicts.cited_b: Net sales divided by selling area gauges productivity of the box.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM: Side A is sales normalized by store COUNT (per_x="store"), Side B is sales normalized by selling AREA (per_x="square foot") -- different denominators/mechanisms that can move independently (e.g., adding smaller stores changes sales_per_store without proportionally changing sales_per_square_foot). Also a SCOPE mismatch: Side A uses unspecified "Total sales" vs Side B's explicit "Net sales." None of the three required criteria are clearly satisfied, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: A fixed household basket gauges consumer price change.
verdicts.cited_b: Shifting spending weights measure deflator movement.
verdicts.reason: Object fails: A is a fixed-basket price index (CPI-style, "fixed household basket"); B is a chain-type deflator with "shifting spending weights" — different construction methodology, not the same metric. Scope also fails: A is scoped to "household" out-of-pocket consumer purchases while B's deflator-based measure implies the broader personal consumption expenditure population (including third-party-paid spending) — different population. Mechanism fails too: a fixed basket (no substitution) versus a shifting-weight deflator (captures substitution effects) are different causal computations, not the same driver expressed differently. All three required conditions fail, so default to DIFFERENT.

verdicts.cited_a: Expenses tied to holding restaurant premises rose.
verdicts.cited_b: Lease and property charges for our locations increased.
verdicts.reason: OBJECT unclear/not-same: "occupancy_costs" is typically a broader bucket (rent + property tax + insurance + utilities + CAM + depreciation on leasehold improvements) than "rent_and_related_expenses," which A's own quote doesn't narrow down ("expenses tied to holding premises" could include non-lease items) while B's quote is specifically "lease and property charges." SCOPE is unclear on both sides -- neither quote states company-owned vs. franchised, or segment vs. consolidated. MECHANISM is unclear on both sides -- neither quote gives the actual cause (new store growth, renegotiated leases, rate escalators, tax reassessment, etc.), just that a cost category "rose"/"increased." Since object equivalence is not established and scope/mechanism are both unclear, default to DIFFERENT per the rule.

verdicts.cited_a: Prepayments on special orders await the arrival of parts.
verdicts.cited_b: Delivery of the ordered item books the transaction.
verdicts.reason: Fails OBJECT and MECHANISM. Side A is a customer deposit/prepayment -- cash collected in advance and held as a liability while parts are awaited. Side B is revenue recognition -- the sale is booked only at the point of delivery. These are different objects (a prepayment liability vs. booked sales revenue) tied to different mechanisms (advance cash collection vs. revenue recognition triggered by delivery). The deposit is a precursor event, not the same driver as the later sale recognition; one is at best a correlated/upstream step to the other, not identical causes.

verdicts.cited_a: Unit sales measure the count of items sold to buyers.
verdicts.cited_b: Dollar sales measure the value of items sold to buyers.
verdicts.reason: Fails the OBJECT test: Side A measures unit count (volume) while Side B measures dollar value (price x volume/revenue). These are different underlying metrics that can move independently -- units sold can fall while dollar sales rise (price increases) or vice versa. Since object identity is already false, verdict is DIFFERENT regardless of scope or mechanism.

verdicts.cited_a: Adjusted EBITDA strips interest, taxes, depreciation, amortization and the named special items.
verdicts.cited_b: The spelled-out measure applies the same exclusions in the same way.
verdicts.reason: Object/mechanism look plausibly aligned (B's quote asserts "the same exclusions in the same way" as A's interest/tax/D&A/special-items strip), but SCOPE is never established: neither quote states the business population or ownership class (e.g., company-owned vs franchised, segment vs consolidated) that the measure covers, and slice_tokens/per_x are empty/null on both sides too. With scope unaddressed, it is unclear rather than confirmed-same, and the rule says any unclear criterion forces DIFFERENT. Additionally, B's only quote is a bare assertion of equivalence ("same...in the same way") rather than an independent description of the measure, which is too thin to compel a merge given the default-to-DIFFERENT bias against over-merging.
